to be working alongside, um, those communities?
Um, and then also I think in terms of the outputs,
have you thought about, um,
how those out outputs will be presented and um,
and analyzed and those sorts of things?
So, uh, yeah, it's a set of questions
that are really helpful for, um, researchers, um,
particularly if they're new to the space as well,
and they just needed additional support to work effectively
with, um, with different communities.
Okay. And yeah, possible we,
could we grab a copy of that framework
Absolutely. Point we can provide
That, absolutely. Share
it. Now, just earlier you mentioned
that the infrastructure that this runs is quite freaky.
Um, what can you go, are you able
to give a similar indication of what that means and,
and if there is an opportunity to update
that given the technologies that are available now with,
without compromise the integrity
of the data sets that you hold?
Yeah, I think it's two things.
It's the, um, the infrastructure itself,
like the IT infrastructure, um, needs an upgrade,
but it's also the way that we do the data processing.
So, um, you'll appreciate starting from the beginning,
the IDI was quite small, it's grown significantly over time,
but the kind of architecture
and the design of it hasn't changed.
And so what happens is three times a year we refresh it
and we, um, receive resupply
of the data from everybody each time,
and we completely reload that data.
And so, um, as it grows and it grows and it grows,
and I think it's 1,073 gigabytes
and 16 million rows in terms of the total volumes
and things that are there now, it becomes increasingly
difficult to kind of run that model.
And so, yeah, it's not just the technology itself,
it's definitely the data processing
and the way that that works.
And so we would like to change the way
that data is processed, um, so
that we could just incrementally update.
So that just means that we're only updating new records.
Everything that hasn't changed, we don't change.
And so that would allow us
to update the data much more frequently.
Um, and so that's a kind
of business case that we are working on.
Alright. Yeah. Okay.
Um, so, um, a note from,
um, the list.
We've got the data in the, the IIPI, um,
only a couple, two or three that reach back to the eighties.
Is there any intention of, of any way, any way
of capturing data prior to the two thousands?
So mostly that reflects, um,
when these data sets collected by the government agencies
that collect them were sort of
collected in digital form in the first place.
So prior to that, everything
that was in here either didn't exist
or it was paper records.
And so there isn't really any ability to go back
and capture the records that were on paper prior to this.
So we are limited by, um, you know, the technology
that was in existence for the record keeping Yeah. At
That time. So there's no intention of
going back
and digitizing that historic information. C
Certainly not by us,
but it may be, uh, it may be something that, um,
the individual agencies, um, uh, have in their plans
and obviously if they were to do that, then
that would potentially make more data available for the IDI.
Yeah,
I've, I've got a question just sort
of from a personal experience
and running how that data set may or may not exist.
Um, having, having worked in the criminal justice sector
for about 10 years Sure.
I I know that, uh, there's a very high incidence
of neurodiversity, uh,
and other complexities in the people
who end up in the court system and the prison system.
Um, in fact it can,
but up to 70% of people have neurodiversity
or having dyslexia, for example.
Yeah. But dyslexia I think is also some
of the most successful business people.
Um, so, so the, well, there's, there's an issue in
that schools may not capture that data at all.
Um, but those that do, I'm wondering how much of that might,
might be in your data sets that, that there's some work
that can be done to sort of surface that more accurately or,
or, or, or not if it doesn't exist.
So I don't believe we have neurodiversity data from
education at this point.
I kind of agree with you. It is possible
that school systems will be increasingly capturing it.
Um, obviously there are neurodiversity diagnoses made in the
health sector as well, so a lot
of those will be made privately or in primary care.
So definitely a gap in the health data that we have is
that we don't have primary care data, um, available to us.
And that's actually a gap for the government overall.
It's not just a gap for us here at Stats
and the IDI, so I would say
I believe health has an intention to come fill
that gap over time, but that is probably the most likely way
that we would systematically get
neurodiversity type information.
I don't know if they're also capturing it in the justice
system, you know, where they are seeing people in,
people are evaluated, um, in that context.
But I would say it's a kind of a, it's a health gap
that we have at the moment, um,
that we would need the health sector to have a way
of bringing it together for us to then benefit
and have it in the start set.
And how, how might that happen?
Like, who would take the leadership on, on saying, okay, we,
we, we think it would be useful to be capturing that data
or, or to be that data in the yada.
So I know that within the health system,
they are interested in bringing significantly more primary
care data together for kind of health planning purposes.
And so I think if they were successful in that endeavor,
then we would be able to tap into it, you know,
through our usual processes, um, to bring it into the IDI.
So I think it's within that health portfolio,
how much progress are they making on that plan?
Questions actually quite related.
I'm interested in new sets
that you might think would be a good idea to capture.
And I can see I, uh, like a, a set
that wouldn't be obvious is actually, um,
employment contracts because it's private information,
but actually what we need to see is the patterns in it.
We don't care about the individual agreements.
And I just wondered what your process was for
choosing those data sets.
Is it opportunity?
Is it, how does that happen
and how much potential do you think there is
in terms of new sets of information
that are you already aware of gaps or opportunities?
Yeah, I think we are aware of gaps, so we're aware
of gaps in an IDI context
and then we're aware of gaps in a kind of more
of a government data system context.
Yeah. Do you wanna comment at all on government data system?
So, uh, so, um, at least a couple of years ago, um,
a data investment plan, um, was put together.
So that was certainly trying to have a look at, um,
more systematically what were the gaps across, um,
across government in, um, social economic
and environmental domains.
Um, so some of that would be relevant to, uh,
finding new data, um, that could go into the IDI as much as
actually we need these data sets
to produce more statistical, you know,
aggregated statistical information, um, as well.
So that's one of the mechanisms by which, um, you know,
we have a, a view of the overall landscape, um,
within the context of our new legislation.
Um, we are starting to work on a multi-year data
and statistics plan.
Um, that's, that's a requirement.
Um, so that will, um, follow on obviously from
that initial data investment plan work.
Um, and I think subsequent
or in, in that same context, we have a range of different,
uh, like we have the information group, so
that's a cross government group where, um,
different data leaders come together, um,
to obviously share practice,
but also to discuss where are the gaps,
what help do people need, um, and those sorts of things.
So that are probably the two key ways that we think about
what those gaps are and, um,
and what might be needed, um, to be filled in future
And directly in the IDI context when it comes to gaps
and potential new data.
Um, so we've got a lot of researchers working in here
and sometimes they'll say, you know, this data set
that we would like to be able to use doesn't exist in here.
And so we talk to 'em about what that might be.
Sometimes they know where that data exists, and so we can go
and talk to that organization.
Sometimes they might not know that,
and we have to see if we can figure that out.
Um, some of the fundamental questions then are, you know,
does that data exist in the form that they want?
And if it does, is it already held by government?
It may, it may not be.
Um, it may exist
and be held by either a organization within a sector
or privately.
And so it's whether or not we're able to access that data
and then sometimes data that people would like to be able
to use in research, um, you know,
is not systematically collected at all.
And so then it's a question of is there enough value in
that data for it to be collected in the future,
which is the kind of data investment plan lens on it.
Um, so I would say
researcher requests definitely drive a lot of our activity
to try and go out and find new data.
And so the more researchers that are clamoring
for a particular data set, the more effort we would then put
into trying to find that data and negotiate
with whoever might hold that data to bring it in.
So we do have a kind of a, a process for trying
to bring new data in on an ongoing basis
and in a way of prioritizing that.
Yeah. Okay. I, there's a supplement actually is, um, so a,
a very, uh, local example, it's of interest
to me is in Queensland Lake District where the, uh,
physics New Zealand data set that is then to inform sort
of population projections have
consistently been undercooked.
Um, it's just been
a challenge that's been there for decades.
Mm-hmm. Uh, local government, um,
has arguably better data sets in terms of
that but's still arguably not good enough.
Um, yeah, because it's, it's very challenging to,
to virtually map what the, what the growth profile will be.
But that has direct implications for decisions around, um,
uh, early childhood, uh, development, primary school,
secondary school roading, uh, you know,
electricity infrastructure, you know, the list goes on.
Um, so, so actually having a good data set is really
important, but more than that is, it's also making sure
that data set is available to the different organizations
who often have their own, um, uh,
projections that they're relying on.
And I, and I think I understand there should be some
reliance on statistics New Zealand,
but when you have a, that, that challenge, so,
so I guess the question is, are you able to, A,
use the local governments data sets?
B is, is there scope to actually have, um,
a more robust data sets that are in real time
that can be used to help inform those planning decisions
that need to be made across multiple domains?
I think your question is probably more about the
production of statistics at that kind of sub-national level.
Like how much do we understand local
and regional communities more than it is about data
that we would seek to bring into the IDI, I mean,
I completely agree that that sort of data is needed
For decisions that you were absolutely laying out there.
Um, I'm sort of wondering whether with the, the information
that's an integrated data set, uh, if there is scope
to have a, have a more comprehensive, um, data set that's,
that's available at that integrated level. So
We do have help inform decision making.
Yeah. So we do have, um, so based on, um, data in the IDI,
um, we've been producing annually, um,
an administrative data population, um, set of statistics,
um, they've been experimental in the sense
that obviously we're still working on the methods, um,
but that, uh, that data set is available annually, um,
and give some insight, not the full insight
that you would get every five years from the census,
but, um, but that's been an addition, um, of a sort
of more integrated set that's been made available in terms
of, um, population change.
Um, and I think in terms of the shift in the census model,
um, one of the, the benefits from moving, um,
away from counting every five years is that, you know,
we'll have annual census information available, um,
in detail, which means that we have, uh,
earlier insight into some of those population changes,
but equally it can be integrated more regularly in
with the data that we already have to help, I think,
provide some of the insights that you may be looking,
um, looking for.
So, um, that's, that's certainly part of our future work.
And just a little bit on that.
So just think about sort
of the vulnerable populations across New Zealand.
Yes. Uh, both, um, vulnerable
because of isolation geographically, uh,
but also say, you know, um,
living in garages, uh, absolutely.
Et cetera. Yeah. Uh, how, how, how much
confidence, uh, sort of can you have at this point,
and in, in terms of the data collection that's happening,
that, that comes in this data set relied on decision makers
in terms of policies, decision making?
Uh, so we probably have some other people
who can speak more, um, in depth about, um,
about homelessness.
What I can say is, so from the 2023 census work that we did,
um, we recognize, obviously we in our collection, um, try
to engage, um, uh, and capture, um, homeless homelessness.
Um, but we also work with a range
of different organizations, um, who, uh,
are more who work with, um, those populations,
those vulnerable people, um, more often every day.
And so, um, through our collective efforts, um, we were able
to, um, subsequently provide, um, an estimate of the, um,
of the amount of homelessness, um, in New Zealand, um,
in terms of our, our work, um, since census
that's really been with, um, housing
and urban development, um,
and other agencies working on, um, how best to
provide, um, estimates of homelessness, um, outside
of the census, um, uh, collection that we've,
we've done in 2023.
Um, but if you want more information about, um,
how we are working in that, um, uh, how are we going
to capture, uh, vulnerable populations, um, we've got people
who, um, could talk to the committee
or provide information thanks.
And that, would that be the same for these, uh, sort of
isolated rural communities or, and sort of households
and sort of, that may not be, you know,
particularly digitally connected, um, but,
and both, both individually,
but also sort of at a, at a governance level,
I think, um, it, it really do, um,
it's somewhat dependent on, um,
how different people interact with, you know, government,
uh, administrative data, how they,
how they show up in administrative data.
So, um, uh, so we do, we do capture a range of, um,
um, cross-section of populations within our, um, uh,
within the administrative data
that obviously feeds into the IDI,
but then also underpins our, um,
popul our broader population estimates.
So, um, it can vary depending on, um, location
and, um,
and different attributes that people have,
um, as individuals too.
Thanks la Yeah, just very quickly through the chair.
Thank you. Um, just in terms of the switch
little from an annual to versus the current model,
um, the insights you talked about,
how variable are those in different, different regions,
different, um, parts of the population?
Um, yeah.
And do you, and, and just following on from that,
is the comparative analysis collecting it
as a one year versus the five year?
Um, so as I said, we, we probably have some people
who can speak far more in depth to, um, yeah,
to our population statistics, but,
but certainly in the context of the IDI, um, being a,
having more regular data available means
that it can go into the IDI and be integrated
and provide insights that you wouldn't
otherwise get if you had to wait every five years.
That's probably the key point. Yeah.
Thank you, Mr. Chair.
Um, just thinking about how you collect
that data from other providers, does the
software hardware make a difference?
Like, is it easily imported into your system
or do you have to manually reenter it
So we don't manually reenter it?
No. So, um, I guess they collect it in their systems.
We work with them to figure out a way
that they can output it in a file that can be sent to us,
and then that file is brought into our systems.
Um, we have secure transfer mechanisms, um, to make sure
that, you know, when it goes from their organization
to ours, it does so safely and can't be intercepted.
But there's a lot of work in what we call data brokering,
which is working with those organizations,
understanding their systems, understanding the data,
understanding the quality of the data
before it comes into our system.
So it's a key part of what we do, trying to figure that out.
Right. 'cause I understand too from the health sector,
especially primary health, is
that GP practices have different software programs
that they use and they don't talk to each other.
Yes. So it makes it difficult to collate that information.
That's right. So I was just wondering if that was a,
you know, applied across different other agencies.
I think it's a effective, for us, it's definitely
effective for what I was talking about earlier about,
you know, is health able to bring
that primary care data together.
So we just work with what organizations have, um,
and hopefully they're applying standards,
and if they are, that's great and it makes it easier for us,
but we, if the data is a high priority, we just work
with them to be able to get it into our systems.
Great. And can you just, um, tell us a little bit about
what the longitudinal business database is?
Oh, the longitudinal business database? Yeah.
So, um, so it's, uh, it brings together, uh,
a lot of our economic and business data, um,
and longitudinal as in, um, shows time series over time.
Um, it's an integrated database, so, um, yeah,
brings those all together
and is available for, uh, people to do economic research.
So some of the work
that gets done in the longitudinal business
database studies on, um, productivity, uh,
aspects of innovation, um, those sorts of things.
So yeah, uh, providing, uh, insights for, uh, policy,
economic, um, policy, competition,
policy, those sorts of things.
And, and what, uh, what does data, that data back
to when, when did it begin?
And, um, and so what sources
of data are you drawing that from?
I don't have that information with me. Yeah.
Um, I would say often the data dates back 15
or 20 years, but not in all cases.
Yeah, we have that information. Yeah.
So we can, yeah, we can provide you,
so we've got a similar picture to the one with the IDI,
which outlines all of the data sets
and how far they go back.
They're a mixture of data, data from Statistic New Zealand,
but they're also, um, a range of, uh,
other government departments as well. Yeah.
So I can make mine start just if you had something like
that, that the database
and there were bits of that information
that weren't present.
So for example, I'm thinking about restraints of trade,
which are covered in the private agreements,
but in fact there's a public interest in what would happen,
how would someone say,
actually we are missing this information legislature.
We really need to change the law so
that we can gather this data for our public interest.
How would that occur? Would it be you as an agency
that said, we've seen this gap,
or would it be the people involved in the research,
or would it be both?
Mm-hmm. Is there a mechanism to tell us that we need
to include in our legislation new requests
for information when that's appropriate?
Um, so I think one of the mechanisms, um, for us
as a department, uh, is we, as I said, like we have regular,
um, we, we have a forum where we get together, um,
with other people from other departments.
And so through that we are aware of different legislation
that's being developed.
And so, um, our role in that is, is to have a look at,
so what are the opportunities?
Yeah, so we'll, we provide, um, comments on cabinet papers
and, and uh, legislation so that, you know, this side of,
um, uh, yeah, this side of, uh, what we do.
Um, yeah, it does recognize the,
the data opportunities, um, within it.
Um, yeah. And, and obviously ministers can make choices
about whether they include that or not.
Thank you very much problem.
Thank very much. It's been
very interesting. Appreciate you coming in.
No worries. Thank you. Thank Terms

Wednesday, 12 November 2025 | Social Services and Community Committee
Briefing on the Integrated Data Infrastructure  
  Statistics New Zealand 

Rachael Milicich, Deputy Government Statistician and Deputy Chief Executive for Insights & Statistics                 
Simon Ross, General Manager, Data Operations and Infrastructure
